answers: Summary - Based on the issues you described, three of the four problems match known incident patterns in our records: DevOps -> TRAIN connection failures caused by ADFS client parameters, external guests losing sporadic SharePoint access caused by Azure AD Connect / duplicate-group synchronization, and desktop Outlook clients stuck in Outbox caused by SMTP AUTH being disabled by tenant security defaults [Data: Sources (11)] [Data: Sources (13)] [Data: Sources (32)]. - The mobile device-management "certificate bridge unhealthy" and the missing meeting-room remote are not clearly represented in the provided ticket data, so I cannot confidently diagnose those from the current dataset. Findings (mapped to your four items) 1) DevOps tool / TRAIN environment (background sign-in works in browser but not for service/account) - The dataset contains a matching incident where DevOps / FO connectivity to the TRAIN CE server failed for background/service logins while interactive browser logins worked. The root cause there was incorrectly set ADFS client parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) causing authentication failures; the ticket was resolved by adjusting the ADFS parameters to match the TEST environment [Data: Sources (11)]. - Suggested immediate action (from that case): escalate to the ADFS/System-Auth team and check the ADFS client registration parameters (EnableJWT, RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) and any MFA-delegation settings. If those are mis-set, revert to the known-good configuration used in TEST [Data: Sources (11)]. 2) External consultants losing SharePoint folder access intermittently - There is a directly comparable incident: external guests intermittently lost access to a project folder because Azure AD Connect synchronization overwrote/duplicated groups (a cloud-native group and a synchronized on prem group had the same name but different object IDs). SharePoint evaluates group membership by Object ID, so external guests (managed cloud-only) lost effective membership after sync; resolution required consolidating groups, cleaning up objects and running a full sync [Data: Sources (13)]. - Suggested immediate action (from that case): have Identity/Entra ID team check for duplicate groups with the same display name (compare Object IDs and "source" attributes), and confirm whether affected externals are cloud-only guests vs. synchronized users. If duplicates exist, consolidate the groups and run a full Azure AD Connect sync [Data: Sources (13)]. 3) Desktop Outlook clients send stuck in Outbox (browser send works) - The matching incident showed Outlook desktop clients (legacy MSI build) could not authenticate to SMTP because tenant Security Defaults (or a tenant policy) disabled SMTP AUTH for legacy clients. Resolving it required selectively re enabling SMTP AUTH for affected users and adjusting Conditional Access policies; tests then succeeded [Data: Sources (32)]. - Suggested immediate action (from that case): check tenant Security Defaults and SMTP AUTH settings, review Exchange logs for 535/5.7.3 errors, and if confirmed, either selectively enable SMTP AUTH for the affected accounts or update the clients to a modern-auth capable build and adjust Conditional Access accordingly [Data: Sources (32)]. 4) Mobile device management: "certificate bridge" unhealthy - I cannot find a ticket in the provided dataset that documents this exact failure (certificate "bridge" unhealthy for device enrollment). There is a portal 502/WAF/certificate issue in one record, but it concerns a customer-facing web portal and a WAF/TLS-inspection header problem rather than a device-management certificate bridge health issue [Data: Sources (15)]. - Because this specific symptom is not in the dataset, I can't conclude the root cause from the supplied records. To triage this you should collect: MDM/Intune connector health details, device-management portal logs, exact certificate error messages, and the time the bridge became unhealthy. If you can provide those, we can map it to an appropriate escalation path. Support level recommendation - Given the examples in our records, the three confirmed patterns above were handled by system engineers / specialized BU teams (ADFS/Identity, BU Infrastructure - Identity, Exchange specialist) rather than first level helpdesk only [Data: Sources (11)] [Data: Sources (13)] [Data: Sources (32)]. - Therefore I recommend classifying this as 2nd level support work (or opening separate 2nd level tickets for each affected area: Identity/ADFS, M365/SharePoint Identity sync, and Exchange/SMTP). The issues involve tenant/AD/connector configuration or conditional-access/security-default changes which typically require system engineer privileges and cross team coordination. Immediate next steps I will perform / that I recommend you do now - Open/route 3 focused 2nd level tickets (if not already done): (A) ADFS / TRAIN connectivity (include sample failing auth trace and service account name), (B) Azure AD Connect / SharePoint group synchronization (include affected guest user UPNs and SharePoint folder path), (C) Exchange SMTP AUTH checks (include an Outlook client log and the 535 5.7.3 events). Each of these items corresponds to previous incidents that required system-engineer intervention [Data: Sources (11)] [Data: Sources (13)] [Data: Sources (32)]. - For the MDM certificate bridge issue: collect connector/portal logs and certificate chain screenshots and open a separate ticket assigned to the MDM/Endpoint team (I cannot diagnose from the available dataset). - Keep the meeting-room TV remote as a local facilities request - not in scope for these IT service tickets. What I need from you to proceed faster - Example failing logs or timestamps for each problem: ADFS sign-in logs (correlation id), the failing service account name for the TRAIN connection, affected external guest UPNs and screenshots of the SharePoint "access denied" event, an Outlook client log and server 535 event for the mail failure, and MDM connector health logs for the "bridge" problem. With those we can route to the exact specialist teams and reference the comparable resolved incidents above [Data: Sources (11)] [Data: Sources (13)] [Data: Sources (32)]. If you want, I can draft the three 2nd-level ticket descriptions (ADFS/Identity, Azure AD Connect/SharePoint, Exchange/SMTP) using the details you provided so they're ready to assign. Which items do you want me to open first?
